name: Greek Titans
shortDescription: The Titans were a race of powerful deities in Greek mythology, the offspring of Gaia (Earth) and Uranus (Sky), who ruled during the Golden Age before being overthrown by the Olympian gods.
description: The Titans represent the primordial generation of gods in Greek mythology, preceding the Olympians. They were the children of Gaia (Earth) and Uranus (Sky), embodying fundamental aspects of the cosmos. The original twelve Titans consisted of six male Titans: Oceanus, Coeus, Crius, Hyperion, Iapetus, and Cronus; and six female Titans, known as Titanesses: Theia, Rhea, Themis, Mnemosyne, Phoebe, and Tethys. They ruled during a mythical Golden Age, a time of peace and prosperity. However, their reign was marked by internal strife, particularly the actions of Cronus, who overthrew his father Uranus and devoured his own children to prevent being overthrown himself. This act ultimately led to the Titanomachy, a ten-year war between the Titans and the Olympian gods led by Zeus. The Olympians, with the help of the Cyclopes and Hecatoncheires (hundred-handed ones), eventually defeated the Titans, imprisoning them in Tartarus, a deep abyss beneath the earth. The story of the Titans represents a transition from a more primal, chaotic order to a more structured and hierarchical one under the rule of the Olympians. The Titans embody raw power, natural forces, and ancient wisdom, while the Olympians represent civilization, law, and a more human-like divine order. The myths surrounding the Titans explore themes of power, rebellion, succession, and the cyclical nature of time. Some Titans, like Prometheus, who sided with humanity, and Oceanus, who remained neutral, were spared from Tartarus and played significant roles in later myths.
